questions.question: In a classic zone-based Angular app, what is Zone.js's actual role in change detection?
options: It compares each binding's current value to its previous value and patches the DOM
It monkey-patches async APIs and notifies `NgZone` that *some* task ran, triggering a CD pass
It marks only the components whose inputs changed as dirty
It schedules a separate CD pass for each component in parallel
questions.explanation: Zone.js is the trigger, not the checker: it patches async APIs and fires `onMicrotaskEmpty` so Angular runs a top-down CD pass. The actual diffing of bindings is Angular's CD work, and Zone has no idea what changed.

questions.question: How does a single Angular change-detection pass traverse the component tree?
options: Bottom-up, child before parent, asynchronously
Top-down, parent before child, synchronously
Only over components whose signals changed, in registration order
In two concurrent passes, one for inputs and one for outputs
questions.explanation: A CD pass is top-down and synchronous: Angular checks each component once per pass, parent before child, comparing each template expression to its previous value. This is what enforces unidirectional data flow.

questions.question: What changed about change-detection strategies in Angular v22?
options: `OnPush` became the default, and the old always-check `Default` was renamed `ChangeDetectionStrategy.Eager`
`Default` became mandatory and `OnPush` was removed entirely
`Eager` became the new default while `OnPush` was deprecated
All strategies were removed in favor of signals-only detection
questions.explanation: Since v22 `OnPush` is the default; the former always-check `Default` strategy was renamed `Eager` (with `Default` kept as a deprecated alias). `OnPush` was not removed and `Eager` is not the default.

questions.question: Which of the following will cause an `OnPush` component to be checked in the next CD pass? Select all that apply.
options: An `@Input()` receives a new reference (Angular compares by reference identity)
An event fires from the component or one of its descendants
An `async` pipe in its template emits a new value
An input object is mutated in place without changing its reference
An unrelated sibling component re-renders
questions.explanation: `OnPush` is dirtied by a new input reference, an event from itself/a descendant, an async-pipe emission, a signal read producing a new value, or `markForCheck()`. Mutating an object in place keeps the same reference (skipped), and a sibling re-rendering does not dirty this view.

questions.question: What is the key difference between `ChangeDetectorRef.markForCheck()` and `ChangeDetectorRef.detectChanges()`?
options: `markForCheck()` runs a synchronous pass now; `detectChanges()` defers to the next pass
Both run a synchronous pass immediately, but on different views
`markForCheck()` flags the view (and ancestors) dirty for the *next* pass; `detectChanges()` runs a synchronous pass *now*
`markForCheck()` detaches the view; `detectChanges()` reattaches it
questions.explanation: `markForCheck()` only marks the view and its ancestors dirty so they are checked on the next pass; `detectChanges()` synchronously runs a CD pass on that view (and its children) immediately. `detach()`/`reattach()` are the methods that remove/restore a view.

questions.question: A developer sees `ExpressionChangedAfterItHasBeenCheckedError` (NG0100) only in development, never in production. What does this indicate?
options: A production build accidentally removed the binding entirely
Dev mode runs a second verification pass and a binding changed between the two checks; production skips that pass so the bug is hidden
The error is purely cosmetic and the values are actually identical
Production uses a different change-detection strategy that re-evaluates bindings twice
questions.explanation: NG0100 is a dev-mode-only guard: Angular runs a second verification pass and throws if a binding changed between the two, catching state mutated during rendering. Production skips the second pass, so the underlying problem is masked, not fixed.

questions.question: When a signal read in an `OnPush` component's template produces a new value, the view updates without any explicit `markForCheck()` call.
questions.explanation: Reading a signal in a template registers that view as a consumer; when the signal changes it marks exactly that view dirty (and its ancestors for the pass), so the view updates under `OnPush` and zoneless without `markForCheck()`.

questions.question: Under `provideZonelessChangeDetection()`, which of these schedule a change-detection pass? Select all that apply.
options: A signal read in a template changing value
`ChangeDetectorRef.markForCheck()` (including the async pipe calling it on emission)
A template or host event firing
Any `setTimeout` callback completing
`ComponentRef.setInput()`
questions.explanation: Zoneless relies on explicit notifiers: signal changes, `markForCheck()`, template/host events, and `setInput()`. Without Zone.js a bare `setTimeout` callback no longer triggers a pass unless it itself writes a signal or calls `markForCheck()`.

questions.question: What is the correct way to state zoneless change detection's status?
options: Experimental since v18 and still experimental today
Stable since v20.2 and the default for new apps in v21+
Stable since v18 and removed in v21
Default since v18 for all existing applications
questions.explanation: Zoneless was experimental in v18, developer preview in v20, became stable in v20.2, and is the default for new apps in v21+. State it as a range (stable v20.2; default v21+), not a single version.

questions.question: After migrating to zoneless, a non-Angular library's callback mutates a component field but the UI no longer updates. What is the correct explanation and fix?
options: Zoneless disables all callbacks; move the logic into `ngOnInit`
The callback ran in an unpatched context so no notifier fired; fix by writing a signal or calling `markForCheck()`
Zoneless requires `Default` strategy; switch the component off `OnPush`
The library must be wrapped in `runOutsideAngular()` to make it update
questions.explanation: Without Zone.js, mutating state inside an unpatched callback no longer triggers a pass because nothing notified Angular. The fix is to drive the update through a notifier — write a signal or call `markForCheck()`. `runOutsideAngular()` would suppress, not enable, detection.

questions.question: In this zoneless component, why does the displayed count update every second even though `setInterval` is no longer zone-patched?
options: `setInterval` is still patched in zoneless mode for timers under one minute
The signal write inside the callback is itself a notifier that schedules a CD pass
The template's interpolation polls the signal on a browser animation frame
Angular falls back to Zone.js automatically when it detects a timer
questions.explanation: Although `setInterval` is unpatched in zoneless, calling `ticks.update(...)` writes a signal, and a signal change is a notifier that schedules a CD pass and refreshes the view. Angular does not fall back to Zone.js.

questions.question: When `inject(Token)` runs at a component element, in what order does Angular search the injector hierarchy?
options: EnvironmentInjector chain first (root -> platform), then the ElementInjector chain
Only the root injector; component `providers` are ignored
ElementInjector chain bottom-up (element through ancestors), then the EnvironmentInjector chain (route -> root -> platform)
Platform injector first, descending toward the requesting element
questions.explanation: Resolution walks the ElementInjector chain bottom-up from the requesting element through ancestor components first; if unresolved it crosses into the EnvironmentInjector chain (route -> root -> platform). The first match wins and the search stops.

questions.question: If no provider for a requested token exists anywhere in the injector hierarchy, what happens?
options: Angular silently injects `undefined`
The search reaches `NullInjector`, which throws `NG0201: No provider for X` unless the request is optional
Angular auto-creates a singleton in the root injector
The component is skipped during change detection
questions.explanation: The injector chain terminates at `NullInjector`, which throws `NG0201: No provider for X`. Marking the request optional (`@Optional()` / `{ optional: true }`) returns `null` instead of throwing.

questions.question: Given this service, what is required for `CartScopedState` to be injectable?
options: Nothing — `@Injectable()` alone makes it an app-wide root singleton
It must be listed in a `providers` array (e.g. a component or route); otherwise injecting it throws NG0201
It must be re-decorated with `@Service()` or it cannot compile
It is automatically tree-shaken into the root injector on first injection
questions.explanation: `@Injectable()` without `providedIn` does not register the class with any injector; it must appear in a `providers` array to exist. Injecting it where no provider is configured reaches `NullInjector` and throws NG0201.

questions.question: Which statement about the Angular v22 `@Service()` decorator is correct?
options: It is a lazily-created tree-shakable root singleton, and classes using it must use `inject()` — constructor parameters are a compile error
It always creates a new instance per component and can never be a singleton
It requires an explicit `providedIn: 'root'` in order to be tree-shakable
It only supports constructor injection and rejects the `inject()` function
questions.explanation: `@Service()` is the modern shorthand for a lazily-created, tree-shakable root singleton with no `providedIn`. Its classes must use `inject()`; constructor parameters are a compile error. (`autoProvided: false` opts out of root provisioning.)

questions.question: A developer moves an `inject(HttpClient)` call from a field initializer into `ngOnInit` and now gets `NG0203`. Why, and what is the correct fix?
options: `ngOnInit` runs before construction; move the call into the constructor's `super()`
`ngOnInit` is outside the injection context; inject in a field initializer and capture the reference, or re-enter via `runInInjectionContext`
`HttpClient` is not provided in root; add it to the component's `providers`
NG0203 means the token is optional; add `{ optional: true }` to silence it
questions.explanation: `inject()` is only valid inside an injection context — constructor, field initializers, factories, `runInInjectionContext`, and functional guards/resolvers/interceptors. `ngOnInit` runs after construction, outside that window, so it throws NG0203. Capture the dependency in a field initializer or re-enter with `runInInjectionContext`.

questions.question: `runInInjectionContext(injector, fn)` lets you legally call `inject()` inside `fn` even when `fn` is invoked from post-construction code such as an event handler.
questions.explanation: `runInInjectionContext` re-enters an injection context on demand using a captured `Injector`/`EnvironmentInjector`, so `inject()` calls inside the callback are valid again even though the surrounding method runs outside the construction-time context.

questions.question: What is the key behavioral difference between `{ provide: OldApi, useExisting: NewApi }` and `{ provide: OldApi, useClass: NewApi }`?
options: `useExisting` creates a brand-new instance; `useClass` aliases to an existing one
`useExisting` aliases `OldApi` to the *same already-registered* `NewApi` instance; `useClass` instantiates a *separate* `NewApi`
Both create a single shared instance; only the token name differs
`useExisting` requires a `deps` array; `useClass` does not
questions.explanation: `useExisting` is an alias: resolving `OldApi` returns the same already-registered `NewApi` singleton, no new object. `useClass` constructs a separate instance for `OldApi`, so the two tokens would point at distinct objects.
